name: Turkish Nazar
description: The Turkish Nazar, also known as the Evil Eye, is a prevalent belief in Turkey and surrounding regions, rooted in ancient traditions. It posits that a malevolent glare, often born of envy or ill intent, can inflict harm, misfortune, or even physical illness upon the recipient. Protection against the Nazar is sought through various methods, primarily the use of talismans called 'Nazar boncuğu' - glass beads featuring concentric blue and white circles with a black dot in the center, symbolizing an eye that deflects the evil gaze back to its source. The Nazar belief transcends religious boundaries, deeply ingrained in Turkish culture and daily life. Methods of protection also include reciting specific prayers, burning incense, and performing rituals. The effectiveness of the Nazar boncuğu lies in its ability to absorb the negative energy of the Evil Eye, shattering or cracking when it has fulfilled its purpose. It's a constant reminder of the potential dangers of envy and the importance of warding off negativity. The practice is not limited to personal protection; homes, businesses, and even vehicles are adorned with Nazar boncuğu to ensure safety and good fortune. The belief in the Nazar represents a complex interplay of superstition, cultural identity, and a desire to control the unpredictable forces of fate. It highlights the enduring human need to protect oneself and loved ones from perceived threats, both tangible and intangible.
subtitle: Protection from the Evil Eye
practices: Performing rituals with salt or lead

keyMyths.title: The Shattering Bead
keyMyths.description: A Nazar boncuğu shatters, signifying it absorbed a large amount of negative energy, saving the wearer from harm. This reinforces the belief in the bead's protective power.

keyMyths.title: The Afflicted Child
keyMyths.description: A child falls ill, believed to be afflicted by the Evil Eye.  Rituals are performed to break the curse and restore the child's health, highlighting the Nazar's potential impact.

sources.author: Nail Tan
sources.description: An anthropological study of the Evil Eye belief.
sources.text: An anthropological study of the Evil Eye belief.
